name: Word Order
summary: The verb-second rule and how time, manner, and place line up in a German sentence.
explanation:
The golden rule of German is that the conjugated verb sits in second position in a main clause. 'Second' means second element, not second word. If you start with something other than the subject, the subject moves after the verb: 'Heute gehe ich ins Kino' (Today I go to the cinema), where 'gehe' stays second.

When you add details, the order is time, manner, place (TeKaMoLo): 'Ich fahre morgen mit dem Zug nach Berlin' (I travel tomorrow by train to Berlin).

In a subordinate clause introduced by words like weil (because), dass (that), or wenn (if), the conjugated verb jumps to the very end: 'Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin' (I stay home because I am sick).

examples.en: I drink coffee in the mornings. (verb second)
In the mornings I drink coffee. (subject moves after verb)

examples.de: Wir fahren heute mit dem Auto in die Stadt.
examples.en: We drive into town today by car. (time, manner, place)

examples.de: Er kommt nicht, weil er arbeitet.
examples.en: He is not coming because he is working. (verb at end)

examples.de: Ich weiß, dass du Recht hast.
examples.en: I know that you are right. (verb at end)

examples.de: Wenn es regnet, bleiben wir zu Hause.
examples.en: If it rains, we stay home.

commonMistakes: Putting the verb third after an opening phrase: it is 'Heute gehe ich', not 'Heute ich gehe'.
Keeping the verb in second position inside a 'weil' clause. It must go to the end.
Ordering place before time. German prefers time, then manner, then place.
Forgetting to move the subject after the verb when the sentence starts with a time or place word.
Splitting separable verbs incorrectly; in a main clause the prefix goes to the end: 'Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf'.
tip: Whenever you see weil, dass, wenn, ob, or da, expect the verb to land at the very end of that clause.
